title: Ukraine Hits St Petersburg Oil Terminal, Khamenei Mourned Amid Power Plays, Dual Heatwaves Paralyze US and Europe
subhead: Ukraine strikes deep into Russia's energy infrastructure, Iran's leadership projects continuity and revenge at Khamenei commemorations, and record heat disrupts life across two continents as geopolitical and climate crises converge.

sections.heading: Ukraine Strikes St Petersburg Oil Terminal
sections.body:
Ukraine carried out one of its deepest strikes into Russian territory overnight, hitting a major oil terminal in St Petersburg. BBC reports that Governor Alexander Beglov described a 'massive' drone attack on the city. The terminal is one of Russia's largest oil export facilities on the Baltic Sea. President Zelensky described it as key revenue infrastructure funding Russia's war effort, signaling that Ukraine intends to keep pressuring Russia's economic lifelines far from the front lines.

The strike comes as BBC Russian documented Russia's escalating recruitment of university and college students into its 'unmanned systems troops.' At least 270 educational institutions have promoted military contracts, offering students one-year terms and large payouts. But the reality is bleaker: at least three recruited students are confirmed dead, and BBC reports that confirmed Russian military deaths have reached 230,407 based on open-source verification, with the true toll estimated between 417,000 and 509,500. GCHQ places the figure at nearly 500,000. Student recruits who signed up for drone operations say they were thrown into frontline assault units instead — one foster mother told BBC her son was 'thrown into the meat grinder' after just three months of drone training.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine's strike on St Petersburg demonstrates reach deep into Russia's energy infrastructure, while Russia's student recruitment reveals how mounting losses are pulling civilian institutions deeper into the war.

sections.heading: Khamenei Commemorations: Continuity and Revenge
sections.body:
Large crowds gathered in Tehran for commemorations of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, killed in an airstrike at the start of the US-Iran war. His body is lying in state at the Grand Mosalla mosque complex, with burial scheduled for Thursday in Mashhad. Al Jazeera reports that Iranian authorities are projecting a dual message of institutional continuity and revenge — framing the funeral as both a farewell and a rallying point.

Former Russian President Dmitry Medvedev, attending the commemorations, called the Strait of Hormuz Iran's 'nuclear weapon' in a video statement, per Al Jazeera — underscoring how the Hormuz chokepoint remains the primary card Iran holds in its confrontation with the US and its allies.

Meanwhile, Turkiye's President Erdogan said Israel must not be allowed to 'dynamite' the US-Iran memorandum of understanding, speaking alongside Pakistan's PM Shehbaz Sharif in Istanbul. Erdogan has repeatedly accused Israel of undermining the deal. A US official told CBS that both Washington and Tehran agreed to 'stand down' after recent exchanges of strikes near the Strait of Hormuz, with vessels now to move freely through the Gulf waterway. Iranian Deputy FM Gharibabadi announced a 'communication channel' with Washington to report MoU breaches, following indirect technical talks in Qatar.
sections.whyItMatters: Iran's post-Khamenei power transition is being managed through a dual narrative of stability and retaliation, while regional powers jockey to shape the terms of the US-Iran standoff — particularly around Hormuz and Israel's role.

sections.heading: Gaza Ceasefire Erodes as Deaths Mount
sections.body:
At least 16 Palestinians have been killed in the past 48 hours in Gaza, according to the territory's Ministry of Health. Seven were killed in recent Israeli attacks and nine bodies were recovered from beneath rubble. Al Jazeera reports that Israeli violations of the October ceasefire have now killed 1,066 people and wounded 3,445 since the truce took effect. The overall death toll since October 2023 has reached 73,090, with 173,550 injured.

Netanyahu said in late May he had instructed the military to expand control to more than 70 percent of Gaza. Second-phase ceasefire negotiations — meant to include Hamas disarmament and an Israeli withdrawal — remain stalled. Trump hinted that Netanyahu may visit the White House as soon as next week, per Axios, which would be the Israeli leader's first visit since the US-Israel war on Iran began, according to Al Jazeera.
sections.whyItMatters: The Gaza ceasefire continues to erode on the ground, with Israeli territorial expansion and persistent casualties making a second-phase deal increasingly remote.

sections.heading: Record Heatwaves Disrupt US 250th Birthday and European Life
sections.body:
More than 165 million Americans faced record temperatures on the Fourth of July weekend, according to the US National Weather Service. BBC reports that Philadelphia's Semiquincentennial Parade — one of the largest US 250th birthday events — was cancelled outright. Washington DC's Great American State Fair on the National Mall was temporarily shut after multiple people were treated for heat-related illnesses, with at least 11 taken by ambulance. New York City hit 100F (38C) on Thursday, its hottest day since 2012. Philadelphia and Washington DC were forecast to reach heat indices of 112F and 111F respectively, approaching all-time records. Trump insisted on an outdoor speech, telling reporters he planned to make 'a really long speech just to show that I can do anything.'

In Europe, two record-breaking heatwaves in May and June have provided what BBC calls a 'snapshot' of the continent's new climate. More than a dozen countries broke their June temperature records — some by margins of 2-3C. Lingwood, Norfolk reached 37.7C, surpassing the UK's previous June record of 35.6C set in 1957 and tied in 1976. Most of England and Wales experienced at least one tropical night (temperatures not falling below 20C), historically rare in the UK. Marine heatwaves around UK coasts have intensified. Prof Ed Hawkins of Reading University said: 'Our heatwaves will get hotter and hotter and hotter until we get to global net zero.' Europe is warming faster than most regions, partly because of melting ice and reduced air pollution, which means less solar energy is reflected back to space.
sections.whyItMatters: Simultaneous extreme heat on two continents is disrupting national celebrations, straining health systems, and confirming climate projections — the new baseline is not theoretical.

sections.heading: Peru's Knife-Edge Election and France's Le Pen Verdict
sections.body:
Keiko Fujimori has been declared the winner of Peru's presidential runoff with 50.135% of the vote against Roberto Sánchez's 49.865%, a margin of fewer than 50,000 votes, BBC reports. The result, announced nearly a month after the June 7 second round, puts Fujimori — daughter of imprisoned former president Alberto Fujimori — back at the center of Peruvian politics with virtually no mandate to govern.

In France, a court of appeal verdict expected Tuesday will determine whether Marine Le Pen can stand in the 2027 presidential election after her conviction for misusing EU funds. The decision could reshape French and European politics, either removing the National Rally leader from the ballot or clearing her path to the Elysee.
sections.whyItMatters: Peru faces a polarized aftermath with a razor-thin mandate, while France's Tuesday verdict could determine whether Europe's most prominent far-right leader remains a political force or is sidelined.

sections.heading: Pope Leo at Lampedusa and Poland's Warning on Russia
sections.body:
Pope Leo XIV visited Lampedusa, calling on European leaders to rise to the 'momentous challenge' of migration. He prayed at migrant graves and the 'Door of Europe' memorial, saying those who died at sea are 'victims both of decisions that were made and of decisions that were not made.' The visit comes two weeks after the EU approved tougher migration rules allowing stricter border controls and broader detention powers. More than 1,400 people have died or gone missing crossing the Mediterranean this year, including 28 children, per the UN's International Organization for Migration.

Meanwhile, Polish Prime Minister Donald Tusk warned of 'critical months' ahead, citing US intelligence reports that Moscow is planning an armed provocation in Poland to test NATO resolve, according to BBC. The warning underscores how the threat from Russia extends well beyond Ukraine's borders, into the alliance's eastern flank.
sections.whyItMatters: The Pope's Lampedusa visit puts institutional moral weight behind the migration debate just as the EU hardens its stance, while Tusk's warning highlights that Russia's challenge to NATO is not hypothetical — it is actively being planned.

bottomLine: Ukraine demonstrated its reach into Russia's energy heartland with a strike on St Petersburg's oil terminal, even as Russia scrambles to replace mounting casualties by recruiting students into a war that promises safety but delivers the opposite. Iran's post-Khamenei transition projects continuity and revenge simultaneously, while regional actors — Erdogan, Medvedev, US diplomats — maneuver around Hormuz and the fragile US-Iran MoU. Record heatwaves paralyzed celebrations across two continents, confirming in real time what climate scientists have projected. Peru gets a president by fewer than 50,000 votes, France awaits a verdict that could reshape its politics, and the Pope chose Lampedusa to challenge Europe's hardening borders. The thread connecting it all: institutional stress — from war casualties to climate disruption to democratic polarization — is accelerating faster than the systems designed to manage it.
